# OQS status meeting – 2026-05-26 – summary

*Note: Generated by AI based on the agenda and transcript.*

## Attendees

- Bruce Xu (University of Waterloo)
- Douglas Stebila (University of Waterloo)
- Loïc Bidoux (HQC team)
- Rodrigo Martín (Indra)
- Hart Montgomery (Linux Foundation)
- Mark Albert (Cisco)

**Date and time:** Tuesday, May 26, 2026, 10:00–10:46 AM US Eastern / 7:00 AM US Pacific / 16:00 Central European

**Location:** Zoom

Information about the OQS project is available at [https://openquantumsafe.org](https://openquantumsafe.org). The project dashboard is at [https://openquantumsafe.org/dashboard.html](https://openquantumsafe.org/dashboard.html).

## Discussion with HQC team

This week's status meeting was held at the earlier 10:00 AM US Eastern slot to accommodate the HQC team. Loïc Bidoux joined from the HQC upstream team to discuss integration with liboqs.

### Bruce's presentation on liboqs/HQC integration

Bruce walked through a short slide deck setting the stage for the conversation:

- **Current state.** HQC was first added to liboqs via Tom Wiggers's PQClean project, but PQClean was sunset in January 2026 and the August 2025 HQC update was never incorporated. Recent issues around older HQC versions (Fujisaki–Okamoto transform correctness, implementation flaws) make it the right time for liboqs to move to the upstream reference implementation.
- **What HQC gains from collaboration.** Access to liboqs's existing testing infrastructure: optimized symmetric primitives (vectorized SHA-3, including a recent AVX-512 fix contributed by Intel), benchmarking, constant-time / memory-safety automated validation via Valgrind, and downstream applications (oqs-provider, OQS-OpenSSH, language wrappers for Python / JavaScript / Go / Rust) to showcase post-quantum integrations.
- **Proposed upstream changes.** Bruce hosts a [personal fork on GitHub](https://github.com) with the changes already implemented. The asks are: (1) namespacing of externally-discoverable symbols (using the MLKEM-native approach: a user-configurable prefix macro); (2) decoupling the RNG so users can pick between PRNG and an OS-provided source like `/dev/urandom`, rather than the current SHA-3-backed PRNG-only path; (3) user-configurable backends for the FIPS 202 API (SHA3-512, SHAKE-128) — during this work a subtle bug was found where `1UL` was interpreted at the wrong width on Windows vs. the GNU/Clang x86/ARM64 toolchains NIST targets; (4) metadata (a YAML file) to plug into liboqs's automated upstream-fetch / code-generation pipeline.
- **The ask.** Merge the proposed code changes upstream and officially endorse the liboqs integration; establish a channel for liboqs to escalate issues that require touching the underlying implementation.

Bruce also asked whether the HQC team intends to update the GitLab reference implementation once the NIST IPD lands.

### Loïc's response and HQC team roadmap

Loïc was supportive of the integration and felt the long-term goals of both projects are fully aligned — the only friction is reconciling short-term timelines. He laid out the HQC team's roadmap:

- **Context.** HQC's implementation was private during the NIST competition (used only to ship NIST submission packages). Once HQC was announced as a standard the team moved to a public repo, but they still run an internal private repo alongside it and are gradually migrating content over, so things in the pipeline are not always visible upstream yet. The public repo milestones give a reasonable picture of plans.
- **Top priority: specification update (target: end of summer 2026; code change at the same time or fall 2026).** The current specification leaves a lot of algorithms underspecified / described only informally. The team is tightening this up, partly to help NIST write the IPD. This is tracked roughly under public milestone 5.1.0. Loïc also wants to publicly float a possible design change for community / NIST feedback before the IPD is finalized, since post-IPD changes will be very hard to land — this is currently consuming team bandwidth.
- **Second priority: quality, security, structure (target: winter 2026 / spring 2027).** ~250 small quality issues in the private backlog; the goal is industry-ready code by the time the standard is published. Security work will clarify exactly which platforms / compiler flags upstream claims constant-time behaviour on (others left to integrators). Repository restructure to drop the NIST-style triplication per parameter set and produce a structure that makes sense for downstream integrators like liboqs — and to produce a portable default implementation. Tracked roughly under milestone 5.1.1.
- **Third phase: advanced testing, formal proofs, more platforms.** A proper unit-test suite for HQC (the NIST KATs alone are not sufficient — Douglas's own work has shown this), tests for malicious inputs, eventual formal-verification work along the lines of what mlkem-native / PQ Code Package have done for lattices, and additional platform targets (NEON, M4-class constrained devices) on top of the current reference + x86_64.

On Bruce's specific asks: namespacing / single-binary-with-all-parameter-sets, decoupled randomness (the team plans to expose an RNG API and let users link their own source), and the metadata file for the liboqs fetcher are all aligned with what the HQC team intends to do anyway. Windows is unlikely to be an upstream test target before the standard is finalized, but Windows-specific changes that make sense will be considered.

### Decoupling integration from the IPD

Rodrigo raised the key planning question: should liboqs wait for the HQC IPD before doing this work, or start now? His view — and Loïc agreed it made sense — is that once the fetcher / integration *structure* is in place (built against version 3 of HQC plus Bruce's proposed upstream changes), pulling future algorithm updates (including the IPD version) becomes routine, exactly as it is for ML-KEM / ML-DSA today. So liboqs can usefully start once the HQC team's summer/fall-2026 specification + code update lands, without waiting for the IPD.

Loïc was clear that before that summer specification update, the HQC team will not be able to prioritize liboqs-specific work. Once that milestone ships, he committed (informally) to prioritizing whatever pain points are blocking the liboqs integration so it can move forward as quickly as possible.

### Platform coverage as an integration benefit

Rodrigo noted that since the HQC team is explicitly *not* trying to cover every platform upstream, liboqs's broad platform CI matrix could serve as a useful proving ground — at the cost of surfacing issues on platforms the HQC team has not committed to. Loïc agreed this would be useful; his company will eventually integrate HQC into its products and will need much broader platform coverage than upstream, so the boundary between "upstream's job" and "integrator's job" is still being drawn.

### Issue reporting and security contact

Loïc invited liboqs to raise issues either directly on the public GitLab or by email to the HQC team / to himself and Céline for more private matters. Rodrigo described OQS's standard practice of mirroring algorithm-related issues onto the upstream repo and asked for a dedicated security point of contact for non-public matters, matching the recent algorithm-team outreach effort; Loïc agreed.

### Wrap-up

Douglas summarized that liboqs is close to landing the current HQC version and will ship a release shortly containing it, which leaves breathing room before the deeper automated-integration work begins once the HQC team is ready to coordinate.

## Status updates (pre-HQC-team arrival)

While waiting for Loïc to join, the group briefly went through the regular agenda:

### OQS Technical Steering Committee

The only TSC activity in the last week was agenda and summary PRs, including the agenda for the June 2 TSC meeting ([PR #287](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/pull/287)) — Rodrigo encouraged everyone to review it ahead of next week.

### liboqs 0.16.0 release

Douglas noted the remaining 0.16.0 items: the HQC update ([PR #2407](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2407)), the FrodoKEM value barrier ([PR #2431](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2431)) — including its associated security advisory — and the weekly build failures ([issue #2436](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/issues/2436)). The discussion was cut short when Loïc joined.
